narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 52% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, your LSAT score of 21, and your strong Year 1 decile ranking of 8th, alongside details like your lower-middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time. Among the factors considered, your LSAT score stood out as the most influential, pushing the model's assessment strongly toward predicting bar failure because it falls well below the average of 37.27 seen in students who passed. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA, slightly under the 3.24 average for passers, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward success, though not enough to offset other concerns. Your impressive Year 1 decile of 8—better than the 6.04 average for those who passed—also helped pull the outlook positive, showing early promise that the model appreciated. However, your Year 3 decile of 5, dipping below the 5.91 average for passers, and your family income level, which is less common among successful bar takers (where middle and upper-middle brackets dominate at over 80%), added subtle pressures that aligned with higher failure risks in the data. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with LSATs in the mid-30s, GPAs above 3.2, and steadier decile progress, your profile signals a tougher path to bar success despite your full-time commitment, which is the norm for 93% of passers. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't factors here—but a data-driven caution based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the passing average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your recent academic deciles through targeted prep or additional undergrad-level coursework might also help demonstrate upward momentum. Reach out to admissions advisors for personalized feedback, and explore bar prep resources early to build confidence. With focused effort on these areas, you can reshape your profile for stronger outcomes next time.
